Devan Amin
Work About Inspiration Contact

I design experiences, systems & stories.

I'm Devan Amin, a designer, storyteller, and future founder studying how people, capital, food, and culture move. My work has taken me from farms in Kenya and agricultural supply chains in Peru to jazz clubs across Europe and shareholder activism and investing at Elliott Management. Across each world, I'm interested in the same question: how do systems actually work on the ground, and how can we redesign them to create more connection, value, and possibility?

Selected Work · 2026 · Design Survivor

Recent projects.

Everything Is a World

Case 03 · Week 4 · Longevity & Repair

The 30-Year Object

I redesigned the sneaker as a modular, repairable system — swap a worn outsole or a tired style instead of tossing the whole shoe — so a $20 part replaces a $150 pair and the shoe evolves with its owner instead of being discarded after 12–18 months.

Case 04 · Week 5 · Sensory Design

Invisible Senses

We designed a scent from Payton's childhood home in Malibu — layering sandalwood, juniper berry, blood orange, benzoin, and vanillin so each note connects to a different part of the same memory, making the invisible place perceptible.

Your 10 Principles

Case 01 · Week 2 · Iconic Beginnings

From First Email to Finish Line

I redesigned McKenzie's Spartan Race as a single, intentional experience — repairing an invisible, stressful "before" and a flat "after" so the emotional arc earns trust, builds energy, and ends in shared celebration.

My RoleSolo · experience designer
Interview SubjectMcKenzie · Spartan Race, Citi Field, NYC
TimelineWeek 2 · 7 days
ToolsInterview, experience mapping, AI-generated mockups
Sensory ThreadHearing — music

Context

The experience is McKenzie's Spartan Race — an obstacle-course race at Citi Field in March 2026. Its beginning fell flat in ways the race itself never did: the pre-race stretch was essentially invisible and stressful. No confirmation email, no QR code to verify her registration, no exact wave start time, and 39 marketing emails. On the day, three separate wristbands made check-in confusing, and an announcer talked non-stop with no music to warm up to.

The Problem

The race had genuinely exciting moments — the headband bib she loved, the obstacle peak — but the overall experience broke down at its edges. The beginning felt unclear and stressful; the ending felt underwhelming, with ID theater at the over-21 check, spectators who paid to watch from stairs with no sightlines, and a generic "pro qualifier" email blasted to everyone. I wanted the race to feel designed from first email to finish line, not just during the obstacles: earned trust before, informed energy during, shared celebration after.

Research

I interviewed McKenzie and mapped her actual journey across three stages — before, during, and after — auditing every touchpoint, pain point, and emotional high and low. The map made the arc legible: a spike of delight at the headband bib and the obstacle peak, sagging into "I think halfway" and a thin finish. I grounded the work in Pine and Gilmore's Experience Economy, which argues that good design isn't just delivering a service but shaping a full experience with an intentional emotional arc.

Process

From the audit I located the real opportunity — the invisible before and the broken after — and prototyped the redesigned pre-race communication as a set of AI-generated app mockups: an instant confirmation on signup, one logistics email a month out carrying the exact wave time and a day-of QR code, and a race-day "Get Ready to Conquer Spartan" screen with details, what to bring, and a countdown. Generating screens quickly let me test the tone — clarity plus anticipation — rather than an info dump.

Key Decisions

Three moves defined the redesign. First, I chose hearing as the sensory thread — music that builds energy before the race, carries momentum through it, and reinforces the high of finishing, so sound becomes part of the experience instead of background noise. Second, I collapsed the three confusing wristbands into one smart band encoding chip, age, and drink credit, so the over-21 check needs no separate ID and entry feels organized. Third, I replaced the flat ending with a community finisher moment and a personal, shareable results email with time and placing — retiring the generic "pro qualifier" blast. Each move ties back to the arc: earn trust before, fuel energy during, share the celebration after.

Tools, Workflow & Takeaway

Tools: A user interview, a three-stage experience map (touchpoints, pain points, and an emotion line), and AI-generated UI mockups for the redesigned communications.

Workflow: Interviewed McKenzie → mapped the as-is journey and tagged moments of delight and frustration → named the redesign opportunity (the invisible before, the broken after) → generated app screens with AI, iterating on copy and the logistics each screen had to surface.

Takeaway: The before/after experience map was the load-bearing tool — it made the emotional arc visible and showed exactly where to intervene. I'd reuse that pairing on any service redesign.

Outcome & Reflection

What changed: The redesigned arc rises instead of sagging — seamless registration, an obstacle high, music and mile-markers mid-race, and a community finisher moment — turning a race with good moments into one coherent experience that earns trust early and ends on a high.

What I learned: The opening and the closing are design surfaces; the race always felt designed during the obstacles, but trust is won or lost in the email before and the email after.

Case 02 · Week 3 · Everything Is a World

Qi Gong: A World Without a Map

I rabbit-holed into Qi Gong — a 4,000-year practice that looks like stretching in a park and turns out to be a whole inhabited world — and turned the dive into a design brief for the person who senses there's depth but can't tell if they've found it.

My RoleSolo · research-led
The WorldQi Gong (气功)
TimelineWeek 3 · 7 days
ToolsDesk & community research, world-mapping
OutputA standalone design brief

Context

The world I went deep into is Qi Gong (气功) — the ancient Chinese practice of cultivating life-force energy through coordinated movement, breath, and intention. It's rooted in traditional Chinese medicine, martial arts, and Taoist philosophy, with roughly 4,000 documented years; the single name "Qi Gong" was only coined in the 1940s to cover thousands of distinct lineage traditions, shamanic practices, medical systems, and martial disciplines that had never shared a name. From the outside it looks like gentle stretching in a park. Inside, it's a whole inhabited world — lineage-trained masters, medical Qi Gong practitioners, the older adults who fill morning parks, Western wellness adopters, and skeptical scientists studying it for COPD, Parkinson's, cancer recovery, and stress while staying agnostic about whether "Qi" is real.

The Problem

The hard truth is invisible from the entry points. By one practitioner's estimate, about 80% of people "doing Qi Gong" are doing gentle gymnastics that only look like it — the movements are real but the Qi is not. Real transmission is meant to pass kou chuan xin shou, "teaching through the mouth and giving with the heart" — not through a screen. And the field carries a wound: Qi Gong had 200 million practitioners in China until the 1999 crackdown, after which public practice was tightly regulated to state-approved forms. The human problem underneath all of it: a sincere beginner has no map. The word "Qi Gong" covers everything from hospital rehab to esoteric Daoist alchemy, and nothing tells them where they are or what they're missing.

Research

I focused on what only practitioners know, mapping the world's people, places, and beliefs rather than judging it from outside. It gathers in person at the National Qigong Association, Shaolin lineage schools, Daoist retreats, and morning parks across China and Chinatowns worldwide; online it lives in communities like r/qigong and r/taoism. Practitioners report results Western medicine can't tidily explain — one recounted a doctor who, months after diagnosing "arthritis and no cartilage," told them: there's no way you should be able to do what you're doing, but whatever it is, don't stop.

Process

Going deep meant learning the framework before proposing anything — the Five Elements (Wu Xing) system, the meridians, the distinction between a movement's outer shape and what happens inside it. From the rabbit hole I synthesized one persona and a clear opportunity, and rejected the obvious "build another Qi Gong app" direction: the field is already full of surface-level apps. I reframed the gap as one of orientation, not content — the missing thing isn't more movements, it's an honest map of where a practice, and a teacher, sits on the spectrum from rehab to alchemy.

Key Decisions

The brief centers a specific person: someone drawn in by a genuine instinct — they felt something shift in a single class, or they're burned out on optimization culture — who has watched the videos and downloaded the app but senses it's hollow and can't tell if they're doing anything real. What they love is the promise of a bodily system that can be cultivated over a lifetime: not hacked, but practiced — that slowness is skill, and that what happens inside a movement matters more than its outer shape. Depth without the performance of it. So the design has to answer their real pain points: no map across the rehab-to-alchemy spectrum; accessible instructors teaching the surface without knowing there's more; lineage learning that's culturally, geographically, and financially out of reach; a medicine that validates the benefits while dismissing the framework; and no honest onboarding to tell a serious teacher from a weekend certification.

Tools, Workflow & Takeaway

Tools: Desk and community research (practitioner accounts, associations, subreddits), a who / what / where world map, and a single-persona design brief.

Workflow: Rabbit-holed into the world → mapped its people, places, and the gap between surface and depth → distilled one honest persona and their pain points → wrote a standalone brief framing the design opportunity.

Takeaway: Going deep changed the question. I came in assuming the problem was access to content and left convinced it's orientation — the brief is sharper because the world, not my first instinct, set the terms.

Outcome & Reflection

What changed: I reframed the design opportunity as an honest map and onboarding rather than yet another movement library — naming what insiders feel but outsiders can't see, so a beginner could finally tell where they are.

What I learned: A mundane-looking world (stretching in a park) hid a 4,000-year, 200-million-person system with a real wound; depth only showed up once I stopped evaluating it and started mapping it.

Case 03 · Week 4 · The 30-Year Object

The Modular Sneaker

I redesigned the sneaker as a modular, repairable system — swap a worn outsole or a tired style instead of throwing the whole shoe away — so a $20 part replaces a $150 pair and the shoe grows and evolves with its owner.

My RoleTeam project · designer
ObjectSneaker → modular system
TimelineWeek 4 · 7 days
ToolsInterviews, exploded-view design, store & service mockups
Principle BaseChapman · Sennett · Patagonia

Context

The brief was to design a "30-year object" — something people would want to keep for decades. We started not with the object but with longevity itself, interviewing people about things they've held onto: 40 years of matchbooks kept because they're beautiful and the boxes change over time; a Singer sewing machine kept because "it still works and I can still use it, why would I throw it away?"; a turtle collection from around the world — the same animal interpreted in endless shapes and patterns; and cast-aluminum calderos that "look as good as when I bought them" because their owner takes the time to clean and care for them.

The Problem

Across those interviews, three patterns explained why things last: emotional attachment and identity (objects tied to who someone was, is, or hopes to be), durability (continued function after decades overrides any urge to replace), and future utility ("I'll need it someday"). The sneaker is the opposite of all three. Americans buy roughly 2.68 billion pairs a year, and the average sneaker lasts only 12–18 months before being discarded — usually because one part fails, not the whole shoe. People spend $300–500 a year replacing shoes they didn't want to stop wearing, and the shopping experience offers exactly one choice: pick a shoe and live with it.

Research

We mapped why the inspiration objects endured and why sneakers don't. Replacing a $20 outsole is far cheaper than buying a new $150 pair, yet fashion's constant churn frames sneakers as temporary rather than lasting. The retail experience reinforces the throwaway logic: most sneaker stores are warehouses with nice lighting and limited choices, with no room for customization, flexibility, or personalization.

Process

We designed the modular sneaker — a shoe built from interchangeable parts on a modular connection plate: a swappable foam midsole with multi-density cushioning layers, a heel-wedge system, a cork-infused comfort layer, a removable premium insole, and a replaceable outsole. The consumer "builds" their custom shoe in-store or online, then swaps individual parts as they wear out or as their taste changes — rather than replacing the whole pair.

Key Decisions

Two moves carried the design, each grounded in a principle. Modularity as longevity (Chapman's emotionally durable design): parts can be kept, stored, and interchanged, so the shoe grows and evolves with the owner instead of being thrown away when one piece fails or a style ages. A circular service and retail experience: a build-your-own store — The Sole Lab, modeled on Build-A-Bear and the Apple Store — positions the consumer as their own shoe craftsman (Sennett's repair as understanding), while a mail-in or in-store trade program recycles and reuses worn parts in exchange for store credit. Durability becomes the selling point (Patagonia): as trends evolve and parts wear, owners simply order new uppers, insoles, or outsoles — reducing high recurring costs.

Tools, Workflow & Takeaway

Tools: Interviews about long-kept objects, an exploded-view product design, and rendered store and circular-service mockups.

Workflow: Interviewed owners of 30-year objects → distilled why things last (attachment, durability, future utility) → chose the sneaker as the anti-pattern → designed a modular system and its retail + trade-in service → grounded each move in Chapman, Sennett, and Patagonia.

Takeaway: Designing for longevity wasn't about making the shoe harder to break — it was about making it worth keeping. Modularity let "durable" and "always fresh" coexist, which is usually the trade-off that kills repairable products.

Outcome & Reflection

What changed: The sneaker shifted from a 12–18-month throwaway into a platform someone could keep for decades — repairing a $20 part instead of buying a $150 pair, and restyling it as taste changes rather than replacing it.

What I learned: Durability is a desirability problem, not a materials problem — people keep what evolves with them, so the design move was modularity plus a service that makes repair and restyling the easy default.

Case 04 · Week 5 · Invisible Senses

A Beachy Wood: Designing a Memory in Scent

We translated Payton's childhood home in Malibu into a scent — layering five ingredients so each connects to a different part of the same memory, because no single note could communicate the full place on its own.

TeamTaha, Sarnika, Payton & Devan
The PlacePayton's childhood home, Malibu, CA
TimelineWeek 5 · 7 days
ToolsScent strips, essential oils & aroma compounds
SenseOlfaction — scent as place-memory

Context

The brief was to design a sensory experience rooted in a real place. We chose Payton's childhood home in Malibu, California — not because it was the obvious choice, but because the scent was unusually specific and potent to her, and because other group members shared a visceral connection to the same smell: slightly musky, the wood of floors and deck very specific to the house, a slight smell of paint almost not fully dry from the salty air, and an air thick with salt and dewy sea particles. The target: a "beachy wood" — the smell of light wood floors and deck combined with the saltwater that permeated everything around the property.

The Problem

Scent is the most direct route to memory — and the hardest to design deliberately. A place as specific as a Malibu childhood home isn't a single note; it's a layered, simultaneous whole. Design it wrong and it reads as generic "beach" or generic "wood." The challenge was to find the combination that communicates this specific place — the deck, the saltwater air, the warmth inside — so that it triggers the right associations rather than someone else's approximation of them.

Research

We started with Payton's description of the place — its musky warmth, the wood-and-salt composite, the way the air felt thick. Then we mapped the memory against the ingredients available to us: what note carries a light wood floor? What communicates saltwater crispness without reading as "ocean candle"? What adds the warmth of interior sun without sweetness? The research was sensory and material — testing combinations against the memory rather than against a brief.

Process

We selected and tested five ingredients on scent strips, smelling each against the memory and then layering them until the combination communicated the full place. The blend skewed heavily top-note because the house smell was light and airy, not heavy or resinous. Blood Orange turned out far stronger than expected and had to be cut in half to keep the blend from reading as citrus rather than Californian warmth. The final five: Sandalwood (the light wood floors and deck), Benzoin (warm, resinous base — sun-warmed wood, coastal heaviness), Vanillin (interior softness and warmth), Juniper Berry (crisp ocean air, sharpness of being right on the water), Blood Orange (bright, citrusy, distinctly Californian and sun-drenched).

Key Decisions

Two principles governed the design. Cross-modal congruency (Spence & Gallace): instead of a single "beach" note, we layered ingredients so each connects to a distinct part of the same memory — the deck, the salty air, the warmth inside. No single ingredient communicates the full place alone, but combined they create something that feels specific and whole. Three levels of product experience (Desmet & Hekkert): the blend had to work aesthetically (pleasant to smell), carry personal meaning (Payton’s specific memories), and trigger genuine emotional response (for anyone with a similar coastal-wood association). Our goal was to design at all three levels, not just the surface.

Tools, Workflow & Takeaway

Tools: Scent strips, essential oils and aroma compounds (sandalwood, benzoin, vanillin, juniper berry, blood orange), and a memory-as-brief rather than a visual mood board.

Workflow: Gathered Payton’s description of the place → mapped the memory to potential ingredients → tested each note individually on strips → layered combinations, smelling against the memory → calibrated proportions (cut blood orange in half) → evaluated at all three of Desmet & Hekkert’s levels.

Takeaway: Designing with scent forced us to treat memory as specification — the brief was a felt experience, not a list of requirements. That constraint produced a sharper result than starting with ingredients and working outward.

Outcome & Reflection

What changed: The blend communicated the place — the combination of light wood, salt air, and interior warmth made it feel specific rather than generic "beach," which was the hardest and most important thing to get right.

What I learned: Scent can’t be designed visually — the only test is whether the smell matches the memory, and that requires someone whose memory you’re designing toward to be in the room while you work.

Case 05 · Week 6 · Your 10 Principles

My 10 Principles

Not Dieter Rams's principles — mine. After six weeks of curating, interviewing, mapping, building, and smelling, these are the ten things I've come to believe about design.

My RoleSolo · Personal POV
SourceCuration journal, body of work (Weeks 1–9), Week 6 reflection & LauRha interview
TimelineWeek 6
FormatPortfolio essay

Context

Through the course I kept noticing the same instincts showing up across wildly different projects — the Spartan Race, Qi Gong, the modular sneaker, the Malibu scent, the podcast. I'd reach for energy as a felt metric (Week 1), map the full arc rather than the main event (Week 2), change my question after going deep (Week 3), design for evolution rather than permanence (Week 4), treat memory as the brief (Week 5), and wonder how to design a universal practice that empowers people (Week 6). These weren't assigned — they kept emerging. The 10 principles are what I see when I read the pattern across all of it.

The 10 Principles

  1. Energy is the felt measure of good design. The first question I ask about anything I admire is how much energy it brings me — not whether it's clever or novel. My entire Week 1 data visualization was a mountain of energy. If a design doesn't move something in the person using it, it isn't finished.
  2. Design the edges, not just the main event. The Spartan Race had great obstacles but a broken opening and a flat ending. Trust is won or lost at the margins — the confirmation email, the finish-line moment, the first 30 seconds. The edges are design surfaces, and most experiences ignore them.
  3. Go deep before you design — let the world set the terms. I came into Qi Gong assuming the problem was access to content. Going deep changed the question entirely: the gap was orientation, not content. The brief is sharper when the world, not my first instinct, sets the terms.
  4. Orientation over content. When people say "I just don't know where to start," the answer is almost never more stuff — it's a map, a way to see where they are and where depth lives. This showed up in Qi Gong (Week 3) and again as the thesis of the podcast (Weeks 7–9).
  5. Design for evolution, not permanence. The modular sneaker doesn't try to be unbreakable — it changes with the owner. Chapman's emotionally durable design taught me that things people keep aren't frozen; they grow. The design move is modularity and repairability that makes evolving the easy default.
  6. Simplicity and energy rise together. My Week 1 data revealed a pattern I hadn't expected: the things that cost more were simpler, and the simpler they were, the more energy they brought me. Complexity is usually a sign that a design hasn't finished becoming what it wants to be.
  7. Make the user a craftsman. Sennett's idea that repair is a form of understanding shaped the modular sneaker, but it goes further — The Sole Lab, Qi Gong's tools for self-regulation, LauRha's "self-empowerment" throughline. Good design trusts the user with the tools and puts them in control.
  8. Layer to communicate wholeness. No single scent note carried Payton's Malibu home. No single touchpoint carried the Spartan Race. Design that tries to do everything in one move feels thin. Layer elements so each connects to a different part of the same whole — cross-modal congruency applied to everything.
  9. Treat memory and felt experience as specification. The Malibu scent brief wasn't a requirements document — it was Payton's description of a feeling. The Spartan Race redesign started with an emotion line, not a feature list. The best briefs I wrote in this course began with "how should this feel?" not "what should this do?"
  10. Design should outlive the designer. The podcast gets handed to DIAD so it can live beyond me. The modular sneaker is a 30-year platform. Lineage Qi Gong passes knowledge kou chuan xin shou. The most important test isn't whether a design is impressive now, but whether it keeps working after its maker walks away.

How I Arrived Here

Principles 1 and 6 (energy, simplicity) came from the same Week 1 data visualization — plotting 20 admired designs by energy, simplicity, and price revealed a pattern I hadn't named: the highest-energy things were the simplest. Principle 2 (design the edges) crystallized in Week 2 when McKenzie's Spartan Race had exciting obstacles but the experience broke down because nobody designed the email before or the celebration after. Principles 3 and 4 (go deep, orientation over content) emerged together in Week 3's Qi Gong rabbit hole — and were confirmed in Week 6 when I interviewed LauRha and realized how much my surface research had missed. Her throughlines of self-empowerment and "energy as the common language of everything" directly shaped Principle 7. Principle 5 (evolution over permanence) came from Week 4's 30-year-object interviews: people keep things that change with them, not things that stay the same. Principle 8 (layer to communicate wholeness) was the entire thesis of Week 5's scent design — no single note carried the place, but five layered together did. And Principle 10 (outlive the designer) became real in Weeks 7–9: the whole point of the "Everything Is A World" podcast was to build something that could keep going after I left — designed to be handed to DIAD with production materials, strategy, and a guest pipeline ready.

Tools, Workflow & Takeaway

Tools: Curation journal (the inspiration collection and Dear Data drawing), project reflections, the Week 6 interview with LauRha, and cross-project pattern-matching across all six weeks of work.

Workflow: Reviewed all work and journal entries → noticed recurring instincts across projects → drafted candidate principles → tested each against at least two different projects to confirm it wasn't a one-off → wrote the final list with evidence.

Takeaway: Articulating taste is harder than having it. Several principles (especially #4, orientation over content) only became clear when I saw the same instinct in Week 3 and Week 7–9 — naming it required the repetition.

Reflection

What I see in my own taste: I'm drawn to design that gives people agency over their own experience — whether that's a modular sneaker they restyle, Qi Gong tools for self-regulation, or a map that orients rather than prescribes. Energy and self-empowerment run through everything. I design for felt experience, not features; I care about edges as much as centers; and I want whatever I make to keep working after I'm gone. The throughline LauRha named — "energy as the common language of everything" — turns out to be my throughline too.

What's still uncertain: Principle 10 — design should outlive the designer. I believe it, but I haven't fully tested it. Handing off the podcast is a start, but it's easy to say "design for longevity" when you're still the one building. Whether the work actually sustains without me is a question I can't answer yet, and that honest uncertainty is part of the principle itself.

Case 06 · Weeks 7–10 · Final Project (V1 → V2 → V3)

Everything Is A World

A podcast for Dartmouth students starving for proof that there are more ways to live and create than the paths their peers are taking — conversations with people who went deep into a world they love. "They called it obsession. We call it a career."

My RoleCreator, host, producer
Co-hostBeth Eagle
TimelineWeeks 7–10 · 4 weeks · 3 versions
ToolsClaude (production plan), Descript (editing), Riverside (recording)

Context & Problem

College students lack direction — not because they lack ambition but because they lack proof. The default paths (Goldman Sachs, McKinsey, Harvard Medical School) are visible; the unconventional ones are invisible. "I just don't know where to start." This podcast is that proof: intimate conversations with people who went all the way in on something they love — a beekeeper, a winemaker, someone building e-commerce in Kenya — not necessarily famous, just deep in their world. The framing that held across all three versions: see the forest, explore the forest, find your purpose. The audience is students who suspect there's more forest.

Research

My thesis: "Dartmouth students are starving for proof that there are more ways to live and create than the paths their peers are taking. This podcast is that proof." I built on Week 3’s rabbit-hole methodology — "Everything Is A World" is literally the name of the Week 3 assignment, scaled into a product. I designed signature questions — not interview scripts, conversation openers: "What’s one thing you believe about your field that most people in it would disagree with?" / "What did you get obsessed with before you knew it was a career?" / "What do you wish someone had told you was allowed?"

V1 · Week 7 · Designing Useful Friction

The First Attempt

V1 was strategy: a one-pager on direction, vision, and podcast identity; signature questions drafted; prospective guest outreach begun; and a Claude-assisted production plan covering recording infrastructure (Riverside vs Zoom — chose Riverside for separate-track recording), distribution (Spotify + Apple Podcasts), pre-production workflow, and brand identity. The useful friction was forcing every decision into the one-pager before touching tools or guests — name, tagline, mission, audience, format, all on a single page. I got feedback from Prof. Eagle, students, and the HCD Committee to sharpen the wording. What didn’t land yet: the visual identity. V1 was all words; the podcast had a voice but no face.

V2 · Week 8 · What Deserves More?

The Ugly Darling

V2 gave room to the ugly darling V1 had ignored: the visual world. I created the full branding — a forest-green palette (Forest, Canopy, Leaf, Path, Mist, Bark, Cream), a trees-and-trail logo with the tagline "Follow the rabbit hole," typography (Georgia/Playfair Display for display, IBM Plex Mono for labels), and podcast page mockups showing how episodes would appear. I evaluated AI production tools (Descript — experimented and loved it; Riverside — set up a demo call with their Enterprise Account Executive). And I secured five guests: Maura Cass (former IDEO Partner), Nina Montgomery (Head of Product @ Ayble Health), Peter Robbie (Associate Prof Engineering), Andy Peay (Peay Vineyards — "From Wallstreet to Wine"), and Tracey Turner (Founding Chair, Stahili — "Building E-Commerce in Kenya"). The "Two roads diverged in a forest" framing became the creative anchor.

V3 · Weeks 9–10 · Less But Better

The Final Edit

V3 was the real thing: record, produce, reflect, and hand off. I recorded Episode 1 with Maura Cass — "Finding Your Throughline" — a Dartmouth alum who graduated in Sociology and Health Policy, spent years in health systems across Africa, walked into IDEO as a design researcher, and spent twelve years rising to Partner. "She didn’t have a plan. She had a thread — and she followed it further than most people are told they’re allowed to go." The episode proved the concept; Maura’s line was: "Design unlocks change." Reflection-in-action from the first recording: great content, but next time send questions beforehand, set time expectations, and do more reps. Then I built the handoff package — production materials, strategy, branding, and marketing — so DIAD can continue the podcast beyond me. Leslie Jennings Rowley came on as podcast adviser/mentor. Five next interviews were lined up. What survived three versions because it was load-bearing: the tagline ("They called it obsession. We call it a career"), the signature questions, and the audience-as-hero framing (Duarte) — the guest is interesting, but the student listening is the protagonist.

Tools, Workflow & Takeaway across V1 → V2 → V3

Tools: Claude for the production plan and one-pager iteration; Descript for audio editing (experimented and loved); Riverside for recording (chose over Zoom for separate-track audio); Figma/design tools for branding. Evaluated and rejected Zoom for recording.

Workflow: V1 = write the strategy (one-pager + production plan, Claude-assisted) → V2 = design the world (branding, tools, guest pipeline) → V3 = ship the first episode and build the handoff package. Each version focused on a different layer: vision → identity → production.

Takeaway: The Claude-assisted production plan saved weeks of infrastructure decisions. The one-pager was the most important artifact — forcing vision into a single page before making anything kept V2 and V3 from drifting.

Outcome & Reflection on the Arc

What changed across V1 → V2 → V3: V1 was a strategy document; V2 was a visual world; V3 was a produced episode and a sustainable system. The throughline — "conversations with people who went deep in a world they love" — held from V1 to V3; what changed was the resolution, not the direction. What got cut: early ideas about also featuring conventional-path alumni. The podcast sharpened when it committed entirely to the unconventional.

What I learned about iteration: Recording the first episode taught more about the format than any production plan could. The plan mattered (V1), the identity mattered (V2), but the conversation itself (V3) was where the podcast actually became real.

What V3 stands on its own as: A fully branded podcast with a recorded first episode (Maura Cass, "Finding Your Throughline"), a five-episode guest pipeline, complete production infrastructure, and a handoff package designed so the show continues without its creator. "Everything Is A World: Follow the rabbit hole."

About

Who I am.

I’m drawn to messy, real-world systems where people, markets, and culture overlap. I like problems where there is hidden value, overlooked people, or a story that has not been fully understood yet — whether that means building a Bambara groundnut supply chain in Kenya, studying agricultural exports in Peru, interviewing jazz musicians across Europe, or analyzing companies through an investor’s lens. Across all of it, I’m interested in how systems actually work on the ground, and how design, storytelling, and entrepreneurship can make them work better.

I work best by starting with people. I ask a lot of questions, follow what feels interesting, and try to understand the human reality behind a problem before turning it into a strategy, product, story, or system. I do my best work around people who are intellectually curious, action-oriented, intense, and willing to push past the obvious answer. I like teams that move quickly, care deeply, and are not afraid to combine creativity with rigor.

When I’m not designing, I’m usually traveling, meeting new people, or chasing music in some form. I love getting lost in new places, talking to strangers, and finding the unexpected connections that make a city feel alive. Jazz is a big part of how I create and consume the world — playing saxophone, listening closely, and following the people and places that keep the music moving.

Inspiration Collection · Dear Data form · 10 weeks of looking

20 designs that inspire me before and after this course.

A hand-drawn data visualization of my Week 1 inspiration collection — 20 designs I admire, drawn in the Dear Data form. Each design is a flower climbing what I called "The Mountain of Energy." The drawing is the felt version of the collection; the table below is the legible one. This is Side A: where my eye started the course.

Legend — how to read the drawing
Position — how high a flower sits on the Mountain of Energy = how much energy that design brings me.
Shape — a single round bloom = simple design; many jagged petals = complex design.
Size — price tier: small ≈ $0–100, medium ≈ $100–300, large ≈ $500+.
Color — primary SEMINAL system: red = S (Service), green = A (Artificial), blue = I (Interaction / Sensory), black = L (Longevity).

The Collection — 40 Designs

40 rows total: 20 that inspired you at the start of the course (Side A · Week 1) and 20 added by the end (Side B · Week 10). SEMINAL = which system letter(s) the design embodies (S · E · M · I · N · A · L · can be more than one). Concept tags are optional — pull from the reference below if a concept jumped out.

Concept tags reference — SEMINAL systems + Week 2–9 concepts (click to expand)

SEMINAL Systems (Week 1)

S — Service
experience journeys, touchpoints, delivery
E — Energy
environmental flows, carbon, power
M — Material
physical matter, form, manufacturing
I — Interaction / Sensory
how designs are experienced, perceived, used
N — Natural
ecology, organisms, food systems
A — Artificial
computation, AI, machine intelligence
L — Longevity
durability, repair, aging, aesthetics

Week 2 — Iconic Beginnings

Structure Mapping
borrowing the deep structure of one thing for another
Far-Field Analogies
pulling design moves from distant domains
Technology Brokering
combining existing tech in new ways
Experience Economy
designing for the felt event, not just the product
Affordances
what an object invites you to do with it
Anticipation as Design
designing the moments before the experience
Controlled Reveal
pacing what gets shown, when
Sensory Arc
the curve of sensory intensity across an experience
One Resonant Idea
the single thing the design is about

Week 3 — Everything Is a World

Disappearing Interviewer
getting out of the subject's way
Curiosity as Structure
the interviewer's curiosity IS the show
Interviewing Users
direct, generative conversation as research
Invisible Systems Made Visible
designing for what's overlooked
Dual Desires
catering to contradictory wants in the same person
Going Deep
rabbit-holing into a mundane domain

Week 4 — The 30-Year Object

Constraints Increase Variability
fewer materials, more interesting outputs
Emotionally Durable Design
designed to be loved long enough to repair
Craftsmanship
visible care in execution
Constraint as Identity
the limitation becomes the brand
Durability as Desirability
long-life as a selling point
Repair as Understanding
repairing it teaches you about it

Week 5 — Invisible Senses

Multisensory Design
engaging more than vision
Frame Innovation
changing the frame is the design move
Emotional Design
designing for feeling first
Obsessive Sensory Attention
noticing what others skip
Place as Story
the site is the narrative
Making Invisible Perceptible
surfacing what can't normally be seen

Week 6 — Curating & Taste

Curation as Self-Knowledge
what you collect reveals you
System Clustering
patterns inside your collection
Body of Work
the curated whole as the argument

Week 7 — Get Out of Your Head

Collective Intelligence
the group sees what the individual misses
Technology as Teammate
tools that share the work
Braintrust
Pixar-style structured candor
Useful Friction
slowing things down deliberately
Candor Without Authority
feedback without rank
Service Design Under Pressure
designing for crisis moments

Week 8 — Ugly Darlings

Ugly Darlings
the unfinished idea worth more time
Artful Making
execution as expression
Design with Intent
every move on purpose
Iteration at Scale
Dyson's 5,127 prototypes

Week 9 — Less But Better

Reflection-in-Action
Schön: thinking while doing
Audience as Hero
Duarte: the audience drives the story
Obsessive Reduction
cutting until only the load-bearing remains
The Edit as Design
what you remove IS the design

Side A reflection (Week 1): Reading back over my list for trends, I realized I had zero items in the Nature (N) system and only one in Material (M) — how a thing is made. Maybe that's because I don't actually know how many of the things I admire are made. I wonder if that says something about the company, or about my relationship to the design and the designer. It was also interesting that as the price of an item went up, so did its simplicity — and that simplicity tracked with how much energy the design gives me. — Devan Amin

Side B Week 10 · After

# Design / Inspiration What inspires me about it SEMINAL Concept tags
21Apple Pay click-to-pay processMade by Apple TeamS · A
22Corduroy jeansn/aI
23Apple TV background imagesAnonymousS · I · A
24ChargeCard wireless phone chargerMade by AquaVault design teamA · I
25I.O AI assistant pin (unreleased)Made by Jony Ive & coM · A · S
26Fitbit AirMade by Google & Steph CurryM · I · A
27Data UI of WhoopMade by Martin Oberhäuser — "love Whoop so much"I · A
28Goodr sunglassesMade by Stephen Lease, Ben Abell & Keri BluntM · N · I · L
29Birkenstocks (Naples & Boston Burks)Made by Karl BirkenstockI · L
30Poland Spring plastic water bottleMade by BlueTriton BrandsI
31Trueffelschwein (men's wear, Berlin)Made by David KeyteS · M · I
32Unkompress Wine / Vinyl Listening BarStan (owner & designer of the place)S · I
33Pharao Sanders, Pharoah (India Navigation, 1977 · Jazz)Pharao SandersS · L
34Tulip silver ring (petal that spins)Mind Your Own Silver (MYOS) — woman at a Berlin marketE · M
35SaunaHouseKengo Kuma & Associates with Vilhelm Lauritzen ArkitekterS · E · I · L
36Girona CathedralGuillem BofillS · L
37Jardin Majorelle, MarrakechJacques MajorelleM
38Circl Yoga and Matcha StudioAnnie MahonS · I
39Bonsai MatchaVan City StudiosM · I
40El món neix en cada besada ("The Kiss of Freedom")Joan FontcubertaN · A · L

What the pattern reveals: Side A was almost entirely I (Interaction/Sensory) — 12 of 20 items — with only 1 in M (Material) and 0 in N (Nature). My Week 1 reflection literally asked: "maybe I don't know how many of the things I admire are made?" By Side B, M jumped from 1 to 7 and N from 0 to 2 — the course changed what I notice. S (Service/Experience) doubled from 4 to 8, reflecting the Spartan Race, Qi Gong, and podcast work; Side B is much more place-based and experiential. But the deeper shift showed up in energy. My highest-energy Side B items are almost all spaces or lived experiences — Unkompress wine/vinyl bar (9.6), Circl yoga and matcha (9.5), Pharoah Sanders's Pharoah record (9.5), SaunaHouse (9), Bonsai Matcha (9), The Kiss of Freedom mural (9). My lowest-energy scores go to the most transactional or utility-driven things: corduroy jeans (3), Apple Pay (4), Apple TV backgrounds (5). There's a clear spectrum from "stuff I respect but use" up to "stuff that moves me to be somewhere and feel something." And cost and energy turned out to be inverted and decoupled — many of the cheapest items on Side B bring the most energy, and many of the priciest bring the least. My eye didn't just move from products to designed worlds. It moved from things to places, from utility to presence, from objects I own to experiences I inhabit.
